title: Plantain chips
subtitle: Crispy fried green plantain slices
excerpt: Green plantain sliced paper-thin and fried until stiff and crispy, then salted immediately. The chips have a clean, starchy flavour with a slight bitterness from the unripe plantain and a satisfying, cracker-like crunch. Far better than most commercial versions. Green plantain is the key, yellow or ripe plantain will not produce a crispy chip.
sourceNotes: Plantain chips (also called tostones chips or banana chips in some regions) are made across the Caribbean and throughout Central and South America. The Jamaican and Caribbean version uses green plantain sliced very thin; the Colombian tostones version uses thicker slices fried twice. Commercial plantain chips are widely available in Britain, but home-made versions from fresh green plantain have a superior texture and flavour.

content.text: Green plantain is non-negotiable. Yellow or black plantain has too much sugar and will burn rather than crisp. Green plantain from the Caribbean or African food shop is what you need, the skin should be uniformly green with no yellow tinge. Peel by scoring the skin lengthways and pulling it away rather than trying to peel from the end.

Thin slices are everything. The chips should be almost translucent, 1.5 to 2 mm at most. A mandoline produces consistent results; a knife requires practice. Uneven thickness means some chips will burn before others are done.

Slice the peeled {{plantain}} as thinly as possible on a mandoline or with a sharp knife, no thicker than 2 mm. Lay the slices on a clean cloth and pat dry. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep pan to 180°C. Fry the slices in small batches for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until golden and rigid. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper. Season immediately with {{sea-salt-fine}} while still hot. The chips will crisp further as they cool.

items.fix: Use fully green plantain. Oil must be at 180°C, below this the chips absorb oil rather than crisping. Slice as thin as possible
items.cause: Plantain not green enough, oil not hot enough, or slices too thick
items.symptom: Chips soft and chewy rather than crisp

items.fix: Ripe plantain has high sugar content that burns quickly. Use only green plantain. At 180°C they should take 2 to 3 minutes, reduce the heat slightly if burning in under 2 minutes
items.cause: Oil too hot or plantain not green enough

items.fix: Add slices one at a time and fry in small batches. Overcrowding drops the oil temperature and causes sticking
items.cause: Slices overlapping when added to the pan
items.symptom: Chips sticking together in the oil

content.text: Plantain chips are eaten across the Caribbean as a snack and as a side with drinks. In Jamaica they accompany rum drinks; in Colombia and Venezuela they are made thicker and twice-fried as tostones. Commercial plantain chips are available in most UK supermarkets, but the home-made version from fresh green plantain is in a different category, starchier, more substantial, less processed-tasting. They are best eaten within 30 minutes of frying, before the humidity softens them.
